In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of pedal point — a sustained note under changing harmonies.

```motoi
(define pedal-bass
  (lambda ()
    (note/strike 'C2 'whole 0.7)
    (base/at-beat 4 (lambda () (note/strike 'C2 'whole 0.7)))
    (base/at-beat 8 (lambda () (note/strike 'C2 'whole 0.7)))
    (base/at-beat 12 (lambda () (note/strike 'C2 'whole 0.7)))))

(define changing-chords
  (lambda ()
    ;; C major
    (note/strike 'C4 'half 0.6)
    (note/strike 'E4 'half 0.6)
    (note/strike 'G4 'half 0.6)
    ;; A minor
    (base/at-beat 2
      (lambda ()
        (note/strike 'A3 'half 0.6)
        (note/strike 'C4 'half 0.6)
        (note/strike 'E4 'half 0.6)))
    ;; F major
    (base/at-beat 4
      (lambda ()
        (note/strike 'F3 'half 0.6)
        (note/strike 'A3 'half 0.6)
        (note/strike 'C4 'half 0.6)))
    ;; G major (tension — the bass C2 is not part of this chord)
    (base/at-beat 6
      (lambda ()
        (note/strike 'G3 'half 0.6)
        (note/strike 'B3 'half 0.6)
        (note/strike 'D4 'half 0.6)))
    ;; C major (resolution)
    (base/at-beat 8
      (lambda ()
        (note/strike 'C4 'half 0.6)
        (note/strike 'E4 'half 0.6)
        (note/strike 'G4 'half 0.6)))
    ;; D minor (more tension)
    (base/at-beat 10
      (lambda ()
        (note/strike 'D3 'half 0.6)
        (note/strike 'F3 'half 0.6)
        (note/strike 'A3 'half 0.6)))
    ;; G7
    (base/at-beat 12
      (lambda ()
        (note/strike 'G3 'half 0.6)
        (note/strike 'B3 'half 0.6)
        (note/strike 'D4 'half 0.6)
        (note/strike 'F4 'half 0.6)))
    ;; C major (final resolution)
    (base/at-beat 14
      (lambda ()
        (note/strike 'C4 'half 0.6)
        (note/strike 'E4 'half 0.6)
        (note/strike 'G4 'half 0.6)))))

(transport/tempo 80)

(base/at-beat 0 pedal-bass)
(base/at-beat 0 changing-chords)
```

A **pedal point** (or pedal tone) is a note (usually in the bass) that sustains while the harmonies above it change. It creates tension when the harmonies don't align with the pedal, then resolution when they do.

;; Overlapping two-voice texture
(transport/tempo 112)

(define phrase-a '(64 66 67 69 71))  ;; E F# G A B
(define phrase-b '(52 54 55 57 59))  ;; E F# G A B, octave lower

(define (voice-one)
  (let loop ((b 0) (ps phrase-a))
    (when (pair? ps)
      (note/place-at 'quarter (car ps) 0.8 b)
      (loop (+ b 1) (cdr ps)))))

(define (voice-two)
  (let loop ((b 2) (ps phrase-b))  ;; enters at beat 2
    (when (pair? ps)
      (note/place-at 'quarter (car ps) 0.7 b)
      (loop (+ b 1) (cdr ps)))))

(voice-one)
(voice-two)

Now we make them **overlap**. The upper voice plays its motif; the lower voice enters **before the upper voice finishes**, so for a few beats, both are sounding.

What does this Motoi Scheme program do?

```motoi
;; A canon at the unison — original melody
(transport/tempo 96)

(define canon-melody
  '((60 1.0)   ;; C, quarter
    (62 1.0)   ;; D, quarter
    (64 1.0)   ;; E, quarter
    (65 2.0))) ;; F, half

(define (play-melody start-beat mel)
  (let loop ((b start-beat) (notes mel))
    (when (pair? notes)
      (let ((pitch (car (car notes)))
            (dur (cadr (car notes))))
        (note/place-at 'quarter pitch 0.75 b)
        (loop (+ b dur) (cdr notes))))))

(play-melody 0 canon-melody)   ;; voice 1 at beat 0
(play-melody 4 canon-melody)   ;; voice 2 at beat 4
```

A **canon** is polyphony where one voice plays the **same melody** as another, but **delayed** in time. "Row, Row, Row Your Boat" is a canon: the second voice starts the same tune a few beats after the first.

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of a two-voice invention with sequence.

```motoi
;; Two-voice invention with sequence
(transport/tempo 112)

(define motif '(0 2 4 5))  ;; a four-note ascending pattern (C D E F as intervals)

(define (play-sequence start-beat root-pitch motif steps)
  (let loop ((b start-beat) (root root-pitch) (step 0))
    (when (< step steps)
      (let inner ((offset-b b) (intervals motif))
        (when (pair? intervals)
          (note/place-at 'eighth (+ root (car intervals)) 0.75 offset-b)
          (inner (+ offset-b 0.5) (cdr intervals))))
      (loop (+ b 2) (+ root 2) (+ step 1)))))  ;; transpose up by a whole step each time

(play-sequence 0 60 motif 4)   ;; upper voice: starts on C, repeats 4 times
(play-sequence 2 48 motif 3)   ;; lower voice: starts on C (octave lower), 2 beats later, 3 times
```

A **sequence** is a melodic pattern that **repeats at a different pitch level**. Here is a two-voice invention where both voices play a **sequence**: a short motif that repeats, transposed up by step each time.

```motoi
;; Simplified bata pattern (secular approximation, one cycle)

(define (bata-pattern)
  (transport/tempo 110)
  ;; Okónkolo (smallest, highest): steady eighth-notes
  (at-beat 0 (sfx 'bongo-high))
  (at-beat 0.5 (sfx 'bongo-high))
  (at-beat 1 (sfx 'bongo-high))
  (at-beat 1.5 (sfx 'bongo-high))
  (at-beat 2 (sfx 'bongo-high))
  (at-beat 2.5 (sfx 'bongo-high))
  (at-beat 3 (sfx 'bongo-high))
  (at-beat 3.5 (sfx 'bongo-high))
  
  ;; Itótele (middle): response pattern (on 1 and 3)
  (at-beat 1 (sfx 'bongo-low))
  (at-beat 3 (sfx 'bongo-low))
  
  ;; Iyá (largest, lowest): call pattern (on 0, 2, 3.5)
  (at-beat 0 (sfx 'conga-low))
  (at-beat 2 (sfx 'conga-low))
  (at-beat 3.5 (sfx 'conga-low)))

(bata-pattern)
```

The **bata** is a family of three hourglass-shaped drums used in Yoruba religious ceremonies (Nigeria, Cuba). The largest drum (iyá) leads, the middle drum (itótele) responds, and the smallest (okónkolo) plays a steady pattern.

What does this Motoi Scheme program do?

```motoi
(transport/tempo 108)

(define (calypso-bass)
  ;; Bass drum: quarter-note walk, but skip beat 2 of each measure
  ;; Measure 1: 1, (2 silent), 3, 4
  ;; Measure 2: 1, (2 silent), 3, 4
  (sfx 'kick) (wait 1.0)   ;; beat 1
  (wait 1.0)                ;; beat 2 silent
  (sfx 'kick) (wait 1.0)   ;; beat 3
  (sfx 'kick) (wait 1.0)   ;; beat 4
  (sfx 'kick) (wait 1.0)   ;; beat 1 (m2)
  (wait 1.0)                ;; beat 2 silent
  (sfx 'kick) (wait 1.0)   ;; beat 3
  (sfx 'kick) (wait 1.0))  ;; beat 4

(define (calypso-rimshot)
  ;; Rimshot on the "and" of every beat (eighth-note offbeats)
  (for-each
   (lambda (i)
     (wait 0.5)
     (sfx 'rimshot)
     (wait 0.5))
   '(0 1 2 3 4 5 6 7))) ;; eight beats total (two measures)

(define (calypso-cowbell)
  ;; Cowbell: syncopated two-bar pattern
  ;; beats: 1, and-of-2, 3, and-of-4, and-of-1(m2), 3(m2), 4(m2)
  (sfx 'cowbell) (wait 1.0)   ;; 1
  (wait 0.5) (sfx 'cowbell) (wait 0.5) ;; and-of-2
  (sfx 'cowbell) (wait 1.0)   ;; 3
  (wait 0.5) (sfx 'cowbell) (wait 0.5) ;; and-of-4
  ;; Measure 2
  (wait 0.5) (sfx 'cowbell) (wait 0.5) ;; and-of-1
  (wait 1.0)                   ;; 2 silent
  (sfx 'cowbell) (wait 1.0)   ;; 3
  (sfx 'cowbell) (wait 1.0))  ;; 4

(base/at-beat 0
  (calypso-bass)
  (calypso-rimshot)
  (calypso-cowbell))
```
Calypso (and its faster cousin, soca) is built on layered polyrhythm. The bass drum walks in a steady pattern.

What does this Motoi Scheme program do?

```motoi
;; Data-Driven Concerto — the score is a list, the interpreter is a loop
(define concerto-score
  ;; each event: (beat instrument pitch duration velocity)
  '((0.0  piano  C4  2.0  0.7)
    (2.0  piano  E4  2.0  0.65)
    (4.0  piano  G4  2.0  0.6)
    (6.0  strings  C3  4.0  0.5)
    (6.0  strings  E3  4.0  0.5)
    (6.0  strings  G3  4.0  0.5)
    (10.0 piano  C5  1.0  0.8)
    (11.0 piano  B4  1.0  0.75)
    (12.0 piano  A4  1.0  0.7)
    (13.0 piano  G4  1.0  0.65)
    (14.0 piano  F4  2.0  0.6)
    (16.0 brass  C3  8.0  0.55)
    (16.0 brass  E3  8.0  0.55)
    (16.0 brass  G3  8.0  0.55)
    (16.0 brass  C4  8.0  0.55)
    (24.0 piano  C4  4.0  0.5)))

(define (interpret-score score)
  ;; walk the score list, schedule each event
  (for-each
    (lambda (event)
      (let ((beat (list-ref event 0))
            (instrument (list-ref event 1))
            (pitch (list-ref event 2))
            (dur (list-ref event 3))
            (vel (list-ref event 4)))
        (base/at-beat beat
          (lambda ()
            (note/strike pitch dur vel)))))
    score))

(transport/tempo 80)
(interpret-score concerto-score)
```

Every concerto so far has been *imperative*: "play this note, then that note." This example is *declarative*: the entire concerto is a **list of events**, and a single `interpret` function walks the list and schedules everything.

What does this Motoi Scheme program do?

```motoi
;; Stretto Concerto — overlapping imitative entries
(define (theme-melody pitches)
  ;; the subject: a list of pitches
  pitches)

(define (play-theme-in-voice pitches start-beat note-dur vel voice-offset)
  ;; play the theme, transposed by voice-offset semitones
  (let loop ((ps pitches) (beat start-beat))
    (when (not (null? ps))
      (base/at-beat beat
        (lambda ()
          (note/strike (+ (car ps) voice-offset) note-dur vel)))
      (loop (cdr ps) (+ beat note-dur)))))

(define (stretto-concerto)
  (let ((subject '(60 62 64 65 67 65 64 62 60))) ;; C D E F G F E D C
    ;; voice 1: piano, starts at beat 0
    (play-theme-in-voice subject 0.0 0.5 0.7 0)
    ;; voice 2: strings, starts at beat 1 (before voice 1 finishes)
    (play-theme-in-voice subject 1.0 0.5 0.6 -12) ;; down an octave
    ;; voice 3: brass, starts at beat 2 (before voices 1 & 2 finish)
    (play-theme-in-voice subject 2.0 0.5 0.65 7))) ;; up a fifth

(transport/tempo 120)
(stretto-concerto)
```
Stretto is a fugal technique where the subject enters *before the previous statement finishes*, creating overlapping imitation.

What does this Motoi Scheme program do?

```motoi
;; Metric-Modulation Concerto — tempo changes via beat subdivision
(define (play-phrase-at-tempo pitches start-beat note-dur vel)
  (let loop ((ps pitches) (beat start-beat))
    (when (not (null? ps))
      (base/at-beat beat
        (lambda ()
          (note/strike (car ps) note-dur vel)))
      (loop (cdr ps) (+ beat note-dur)))))

(define (metric-modulation-concerto)
  ;; section 1: tempo 60, quarter-notes
  (transport/tempo 60)
  (play-phrase-at-tempo '(C4 D4 E4 F4) 0.0 1.0 0.7)
  
  ;; modulation 1: old eighth = new quarter, so new tempo = 60 * 1.5 = 90
  (base/at-beat 4.0
    (lambda () (transport/tempo 90)))
  (play-phrase-at-tempo '(G4 A4 B4 C5) 4.0 1.0 0.75)
  
  ;; modulation 2: old eighth = new quarter, so new tempo = 90 * 1.5 = 135
  (base/at-beat 8.0
    (lambda () (transport/tempo 135)))
  (play-phrase-at-tempo '(C5 B4 A4 G4) 8.0 1.0 0.8)
  
  ;; modulation 3: old eighth = new quarter, so new tempo = 135 * 1.5 = 202.5
  (base/at-beat 12.0
    (lambda () (transport/tempo 202)))
  (play-phrase-at-tempo '(F4 E4 D4 C4) 12.0 1.0 0.85))

(metric-modulation-concerto)
```
Metric modulation is a tempo technique where the beat subdivides (or multiplies) to create a new tempo that's mathematically related to the old tempo.
How do I work with strings for the concerto in Motoi?

```motoi
;; Rachmaninoff Concerto No. 2, Movement I, mm. 50–58
;; First theme: clarinet melody (Eb major) with pizzicato strings

(define (section-voice-clarinet)
  ;; Warm, reedy harmonic profile (fundamental + strong 3rd/5th harmonics)
  ;; [needs: GAP-6 — createPeriodicWave section-voice tuning]
  ;; For now, simulate with a single note + implied timbre
  'clarinet-voice)

(define (pizzicato-envelope pitch time velocity)
  ;; Short attack, instant decay, no sustain (plucked string)
  ;; [needs: GAP-3 — ADSR envelope control]
  ;; Simulate with very short duration
  (note/strike pitch time 0.15 velocity))

(define (first-theme)
  (let ((tempo 72)  ; bpm, Moderato
        (beat (/ 60.0 72)))
    ;; Clarinet melody (voice implied)
    (note/strike 67 (* 0 beat) (* 1 beat) 0.70)  ; G4 quarter
    (note/strike 70 (* 1 beat) (* 1 beat) 0.72)  ; Bb4 quarter
    (note/strike 72 (* 2 beat) (* 2 beat) 0.75)  ; C5 half
    (note/strike 70 (* 4 beat) (* 1 beat) 0.73)  ; Bb4 quarter
    (note/strike 68 (* 5 beat) (* 1 beat) 0.71)  ; Ab4 quarter
    (note/strike 67 (* 6 beat) (* 1 beat) 0.70)  ; G4 quarter
    (note/strike 65 (* 7 beat) (* 1 beat) 0.68)  ; F4 quarter
    (note/strike 63 (* 8 beat) (* 2 beat) 0.75)  ; Eb4 half
    (note/strike 65 (* 10 beat) (* 1 beat) 0.70) ; F4 quarter
    (note/strike 67 (* 11 beat) (* 1 beat) 0.72) ; G4 quarter
    ;; Ritardando in bar 4 (stretch the last two beats by 1.2x)
    (note/strike 68 (* 12 beat) (* 3 beat 1.2) 0.80) ; Ab4 dotted-half, rit
    (note/strike 67 (* 15 beat 1.2) (* 1 beat 1.3) 0.78) ; G4 quarter, held
    
    ;; Pizzicato strings (Eb2–Bb2–G3 triad, eighth-note pulse, 16 beats)
    (let loop ((b 0))
      (when (< b 16)
        (pizzicato-envelope 39 (* b beat 0.5) 0.55)  ; Eb2
        (pizzicato-envelope 46 (* b beat 0.5) 0.50)  ; Bb2
        (pizzicato-envelope 55 (* b beat 0.5) 0.48)  ; G3
        (loop (+ b 1))))))

(first-theme)
```

After the opening chords and a long orchestral buildup, the *first theme* enters at measure 50: a lyrical, yearning melody in E-flat major, first stated by **solo clarinet** (doubled by oboe), accompanied by **pizzicato strings** (short, plucked notes).
In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of polyphonic texture: three independent lines in the coda.

```motoi
;; Rachmaninoff Concerto No. 2, Movement I (Coda)
;; Polyphonic texture: three independent lines (piano RH, piano LH, cello)

(define (polyphonic-coda)
  (let ((tempo 96)
        (beat (/ 60.0 96)))
    ;; Piano right hand: descending chromatic line (C5 → C4, 13 notes, eighth-notes)
    (let loop ((pitch 72) (t 0.0))
      (when (>= pitch 60)
        (note/strike pitch t (* 0.5 beat) 0.75)
        (loop (- pitch 1) (+ t (* 0.5 beat)))))
    
    ;; Piano left hand: arpeggiated C minor triad (C2 Eb2 G2 C3, quarter-notes, offset by 1 beat)
    (note/strike 36 (* 1 beat) beat 0.70)   ; C2
    (note/strike 39 (* 2 beat) beat 0.70)   ; Eb2
    (note/strike 43 (* 3 beat) beat 0.70)   ; G2
    (note/strike 48 (* 4 beat) beat 0.70)   ; C3
    (note/strike 36 (* 5 beat) beat 0.70)   ; C2 (repeat)
    
    ;; Cello: sustained G2 (whole-note, pp, underneath both piano lines)
    (note/strike 43 0.0 (* 8 beat) 0.50)))

(polyphonic-coda)
```

The final example is a **polyphonic texture** from the Moderato coda: three independent melodic lines (piano right hand, piano left hand, cello) that move simultaneously but do not align rhythmically.

What does this Motoi Scheme program do?

```motoi
(define (bell-modes f0 count)
  (map (lambda (n)
         (* f0 (case n
                 ((0) 1.0)
                 ((1) 2.4)
                 ((2) 4.0)
                 ((3) 5.4)
                 ((4) 7.0)
                 (else (* n 1.5)))))
       (iota count)))

(define (bell-decays count)
  (map (lambda (n) (- 3.0 (* n 0.3))) (iota count)))

(define (bell-amps count)
  (map (lambda (n) (/ 1.0 (+ 1.0 n))) (iota count)))

(strike-modal (bell-modes 220 5)
              (bell-decays 5)
              (bell-amps 5))
```
Real bells follow a pattern: the modes are near (but not exactly) `f0, 2.4·f0, 4·f0, 5.4·f0, ...`. You can generate a mode list programmatically:

(pluck-with-body 220 2.0)

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of a locked groove.

```motoi
;; A perfectly locked one-bar groove
(tempo 120)
(define (locked-loop)
  (schedule (tone 60 0.2) :at 0)
  (schedule (tone 200 0.1) :at 1)
  (schedule (tone 60 0.2) :at 2)
  (schedule (tone 200 0.1) :at 3)
  ;; loop jumps from beat 4 back to beat 0
  (transport/loop :start 0 :end 4 :count :infinite))

(locked-loop)
```

A **locked groove** is a pattern that loops perfectly, with no drift, no gap, no click. The last event in the loop lands exactly one bar before the first event.

In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of aliasing: frequencies above nyquist fold back.

```motoi
; if sample rate = 44100 Hz, Nyquist = 22050 Hz
; a 23000 Hz tone would alias to 44100 - 23000 = 21100 Hz
(let ((too-high (tone 23000 0.3)))  ; above Nyquist if sr=44100
  (spectrum-of too-high))
```

If you try to synthesize a frequency above half the sample rate (the Nyquist limit), it *aliases*—appears as a lower, false frequency in the spectrum.

    (if (= i n) (reverse acc)

                  (transpose melody ratio)))
              (iota count))))

What does this Motoi Scheme program do?

```motoi
;; sixteen-step kick: four-on-the-floor
(define kick-16 '(#t #f #f #f  #t #f #f #f  #t #f #f #f  #t #f #f #f))

;; sixteen-step snare: backbeat on 4 and 12
(define snare-16 '(#f #f #f #t  #f #f #f #f  #f #f #f #t  #f #f #f #f))

;; sixteen-step hat: steady eighths
(define hat-16 '(#t #f #t #f  #t #f #t #f  #t #f #t #f  #t #f #t #f))

(define (emit-pattern-schedule tracks step-dur)
  (for-each
    (lambda (track)
      (let ((name (car track))
            (pattern (cadr track))
            (sound (caddr track)))
        (display (string-append "\n;; " name "\n"))
        (let loop ((steps pattern) (beat 0))
          (when (not (null? steps))
            (when (car steps)
              (display "(schedule (:at ")
              (display beat)
              (display " ")
              (display sound)
              (display "))\n"))
            (loop (cdr steps) (+ beat step-dur))))))
    tracks))

(emit-pattern-schedule
  (list
    (list "kick" kick-16 "(tone 60 0.3)")
    (list "snare" snare-16 "(noise 'white)")
    (list "hat" hat-16 "(filter 'highpass (noise 'white) :cutoff 8000)"))
  0.25)
```
Now we scale to sixteen steps—the classic 808/909 layout.
In Motoi Scheme, show me an example of euclidean rhythms — distribute n hits over m steps.

```motoi
;; euclidean rhythm: distribute k hits over n steps
(define (euclidean k n)
  (let ((pattern (make-list n #f)))
    (let loop ((i 0) (hits k) (acc pattern))
      (if (>= i n)
          acc
          (let* ((step-val (>= (* hits n) (* k i)))
                 (new-hits (if step-val (- hits 1) hits)))
            (loop (+ i 1) new-hits
                  (append (take acc i)
                          (list step-val)
                          (drop acc (+ i 1)))))))))

;; 5 hits in 8 steps
(euclidean 5 8)
;; => (#t #f #t #t #f #t #t #f)  ;; close to a clave
```

Euclidean rhythms are the even-as-possible distribution of onsets—ancient bell patterns, clave, tresillo.

What does this Motoi Scheme program do?

```motoi
;; run-length encode a pattern
(define (rle pattern)
  (if (null? pattern)
      '()
      (let loop ((rest (cdr pattern)) (current (car pattern)) (count 1) (acc '()))
        (cond
          ((null? rest)
           (reverse (cons (cons count current) acc)))
          ((equal? (car rest) current)
           (loop (cdr rest) current (+ count 1) acc))
          (else
           (loop (cdr rest) (car rest) 1 (cons (cons count current) acc)))))))

(rle '(#t #t #t #f #f #t))
;; => ((3 . #t) (2 . #f) (1 . #t))
```
Instead of `(#t #t #t #t #f #f)`, we can write `((4 . #t) (2 .

shuffle a list

Fisher-Yates. Or a quick-and-dirty sort by random keys:

```motoi
(define (shuffle lst)
  (map cdr
       (sort (lambda (a b) (< (car a) (car b)))
             (map (lambda (x) (cons (random 1000000) x)) lst))))
```

draw pong

Classic setup — two paddles, a ball, bounce logic:

```motoi
(define ball-x 60) (define ball-y 40)
(define ball-dx 2) (define ball-dy 1)
(define left-y 30) (define right-y 30)

(on-frame
  (lambda ()
    (fb/clear)
    (set! ball-x (+ ball-x ball-dx))
    (set! ball-y (+ ball-y ball-dy))
    (fb/rect 5 left-y 3 15 'white)
    (fb/rect 112 right-y 3 15 'white)
    (fb/circle ball-x ball-y 2 'white)))
```
